title: Evenings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Warren pauses data centers after storms knock out power; pushback grows statewide.

lines.text: Good evening, and welcome to Evenings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
Tonight we're diving into Warren's surprise moratorium on new data centers after those July storms took down more than sixty power lines.
That sounds like a direct response to real infrastructure problems. What exactly happened in Warren?
City council approved a temporary freeze right after the storms showed how fragile the aging grid already is. Attorneys are now drafting the pause while they rewrite zoning rules.
So this isn't just abstract worry — the storms actually knocked out service. How long could this moratorium last?
It's temporary by design, meant to give time to update the rules before any new projects move forward.
I wonder how this fits into the bigger picture across the state. We've seen protests in Vicksburg and Augusta Township too.
Exactly. Warren's move is the latest in a string of local pushbacks. Residents in Kalamazoo County turned out this week over noise, traffic, and utility strain.
What do these projects actually do to everyday life? Power bills, water use, farmland — those are the things neighbors keep bringing up.
Data centers can pull enormous amounts of electricity and water. In farming areas like Saline Township, folks worry about draining supplies and changing the rural character.
And the tax side? I saw something about Oracle getting an unlimited break in the Ann Arbor area.
Yes, Saline Township lifted the dollar cap on incentives for that big Oracle-OpenAI campus. That could mean decades of lost revenue for schools and roads.
How did that even happen? Was there a vote or just a reversal?
It was a change in the proposal — they dropped the earlier cap. Residents are now looking at potentially unlimited tax breaks paid for locally.
Meanwhile in Warren the focus is purely on keeping the lights on. Is the grid statewide ready for this kind of growth?
That's the open question. Utilities are already fielding questions about serving a major Microsoft AI campus in Kent County at a hearing this week.
Neighbors there are asking the same things: will our bills go up? Will the water hold out?
Right. And Hagar Township just approved its own one-year ban to study those exact impacts before anything advances.
It feels like townships are scrambling for tools while the state level is stalled. What's going on with that guardrail bill?
The House Speaker is holding up legislation that would set statewide rules on siting, power use, and tax breaks. Sponsors say the delay leaves local governments without clear guidance.
Bipartisan support before the hold, too. So now every township is kind of on its own figuring this out.
That's the frustration. Without state guardrails, places like Warren are creating their own pauses while others push forward.
What happens next in Warren specifically? Do they have a timeline for the new zoning?
City attorneys are drafting the moratorium language now. The goal is to revise the rules so future projects don't overload the system the way the storms revealed.
And how are residents reacting there? Any organized pushback like in Vicksburg?
The council acted quickly after the storms, so the move seems to reflect local concerns about reliability. More actions are planned elsewhere if proposals keep advancing.
Over in Genesee County they're watching New York's moratorium. Could that affect the STAMP project?
Local officials are concerned it might delay or block plans that were counting on jobs and tax base growth. Hundreds of construction and operations positions are on the line.
So it's not just Michigan making these calls — neighboring states' decisions ripple over here too.
Absolutely. The competitive edge for sites can shift fast depending on what other states do.
Back to the tax side — with the Oracle deal now uncapped, what does that mean for Washtenaw County taxpayers long term?
It shifts the financial stakes. Property-tax revenue that would have gone to schools and roads could be reduced for years with no upper limit on the abatement.
And the project itself is already under construction, right? So the resistance is happening while work is moving ahead.
Yes. Saline Township residents, a community of about twenty-four hundred, are speaking out about water, costs, and keeping the rural feel.
Augusta Township has a referendum coming up next month after weeks of protests. Voters will decide whether to allow a large project near Ann Arbor.
That vote comes after heated campaigning. One resident even urged officials to ignore social media pressure in the final stretch.
It really shows how these decisions are landing at the ballot box now.
And the utility hearing in Kent County this week will be another chance for neighbors to ask direct questions about power and water for that Microsoft campus.
What should listeners watch for at that hearing?
Details on how the utility plans to serve the load without straining the local grid or supplies. It's one of the first major AI facilities in west Michigan.
Any other quick developments we should hit before we wrap the big picture?
New York's pause on large data centers is the one to watch for Genesee County. It could change the outlook for the STAMP site.
And Hagar Township's year-long ban gives residents breathing room to study the impacts.
Right. Multiple townships are hitting pause while the state bill sits.
On deck, what's coming up that people can actually attend?
The utility's public hearing on serving the Microsoft AI campus in Kent County is this week — that's the one for west Michigan neighbors to weigh in.
And the Augusta Township referendum is next month. Residents there will vote directly on the data center proposal.
Those are the two concrete dates listeners can mark. Check local notices for exact times and locations.
Anything else on the calendar we should flag?
Not at the moment — the tracker will update if new meetings pop up.
All right, so the through-line tonight is local governments stepping in where state rules are stalled.
And residents making their voices heard on power, water, taxes, and community character.
It'll be interesting to see how the Warren moratorium shapes up and whether other places follow.
We'll keep tracking it. Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker.
We're back tomorrow.
Thanks for listening tonight.

episode_title: Warren Freezes New Data Centers After Grid Strain Exposed by Storms
